title: Paul Sculthorpe

rugbyleagueproject.org|publisher=rugbyleagueproject.org|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}{{Rugby league records |id=1378 |title=Paul Sculthorpe |access-date=24 January 2026}} | module = {{infobox person | embed = yes | father = | mother = | spouse = | education = [[Counthill School]] | alma_mater = | relatives = [[Danny Sculthorpe]] (brother) }} }} '''Paul Sculthorpe''' [[Member of the Most Excellent Order of the British Empire|MBE]] (born 22 September 1977) is an English former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|lf|so}} or {{rlp|sr}} forward in the 1990s and 2000s. He began his club career at the [[Warrington Wolves]] before joining [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]], with whom he won four [[Super League Grand Final|Grand Final]]s, four [[Challenge Cup]]s and two [[World Club Challenge]]s, as well as becoming the first player to win the [[Man of Steel Award]] in two consecutive seasons. He played for [[England national rugby league team|England]] and [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] at international level, as well as representing [[Lancashire rugby league team|Lancashire]]. He is the older brother of former player [[Danny Sculthorpe]]. ==Early years== Sculthorpe was born on 22 September 1977 in [[Burnley]], [[Lancashire]], England. He grew up in [[Oldham]], and was educated at Thorp County Primary School, Royton until the age of 10, completing his primary education at Watersheddings Primary School and then [[Counthill School]]. He played junior rugby league for Royton Tigers,{{Cite web |date=2012-07-18 |title=Paul Sculthorpe |url=https://rlbase.wordpress.com/rl-role-models/paul-sculthorpe/ |access-date=2024-06-14 |website=RUGBY LEAGUE BASE |language=en}} then Oldham Juniors, Mayfield and Waterhead.{{Cite web |title=Paul Sculthorpe - What does Paul mean for rugby league? |url=https://paulsculthorpe.co.uk/paul-sculthorpe/ |access-date=2024-06-14 |website=paulsculthorpe.co.uk |language=en-US}}{{cite web|title=Club Of The Week | Waterhead Warriors|url=http://www.rugby-league.com/article/38541/club-of-the-week--waterhead-warriors|publisher=The Rugby Football League|access-date=10 December 2017}} He moved to Ince Rosebridge after a disagreement with his teammates regarding a Southern Hemisphere tour. He played for the Wigan club for two years, then started his professional career with [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]], making his debut in 1995.{{cite web|last1=Wilson|first1=Andy|title=Saints seethe at double whammy|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2006/mar/01/rugbyleague.leedsrhinos|website=The Guardian|access-date=10 December 2017|date=1 March 2006}} In 1996, he was named in the inaugural [[Super League Dream Team]], and in the post season he went on the [[1996 Great Britain Lions tour]] of Oceania.{{cite news |last = Hadfield |first = Dave |title = Bell's scouting trip off |work = [[The Independent]] |publisher = independent.co.uk |date = 1996-08-30 |url = https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/bells-scouting-trip-off-1312189.html |access-date=6 November 2013 |location=London, England}} Sculthorpe moved to [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] in 1997 for £375,000,{{cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/5107598.stm|title=Offiah hails arrival of Fielden|publisher=bbc.co.uk|date=2006-06-22|access-date=1 January 2009|first=Julian|last=Shea}} at the time a record transfer fee for a forward (based on increases in average earnings, this would be approximately £745,690.28 in 2025).{{cite web|url=http://www.measuringworth.com/ukcompare|title=Measuring Worth - Relative Value of UK Pounds|publisher=Measuring Worth|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015}} In the 1997 post season, Sculthorpe was selected to play for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] at loose forward in all three matches of the [[Super League Test series]] against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]]. ==St Helens== Sculthorpe played for St Helens as a {{rlp|so}} in their [[1999 Super League Grand Final]] victory over [[Bradford Bulls]]. Having won the 1999 Championship, St Helens contested in the [[2000 World Club Challenge]] against [[National Rugby League]] Premiers [[Melbourne Storm]], with Sculthorpe playing as a loose forward in the loss. He also played at loose forward and kicked a drop goal in their [[2000 Super League Grand Final]] victory over [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]. As [[Super League V]] champions, St Helens played against [[2000 NRL season|2000 NRL]] Premiers [[Brisbane Broncos]] in the [[2001 World Club Challenge]]. Sculthorpe played at loose forward, scoring a try and kicking a drop goal in Saints' victory. Sculthorpe was named as [[Man of Steel Award|Man of Steel]] in 2001 and 2002. He was Saints’ sole representative in the 2002 [[Super League Dream Team]], a feat he repeated in 2004.{{Citation needed|date=August 2008}} Sculthorpe played for St Helens at stand-off in their [[2002 Super League Grand Final]] victory against Bradford. Having won [[Super League VI]], St Helens contested the [[2003 World Club Challenge]] against 2002 NRL Premiers [[Sydney Roosters]]. Sculthorpe played at loose forward in Saints' 38–0 loss. Sculthorpe was named St Helens captain in 2004, and led the team to victory over Wigan in the [[2004 Challenge Cup]] Final at the [[Millennium Stadium]], Cardiff. That year he also joined a select band of players to have scored 100 tries and 100 goals in Super League. In the end of season [[2004 Rugby League Tri-Nations]] tournament, in the final against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] he played at loose forward in the Lions' 44–4 loss.{{Cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/4046521.stm|title = Great Britain 4-44 Australia|date = 27 November 2004}} In 2005 Sculthorpe scored his 1,000th point for Saints.{{Citation needed|date=August 2008}}He was named as Great Britain captain in 2005, although injury ruled out for the most of 2005's [[Super League X]] and prevented him from leading the side in the [[2005 Tri-Nations]] tournament. As one of the stars of the world game, Sculthorpe secured a lucrative sponsorship deal as the face of [[Global Gillette|Gillette]], which at the time was the most lucrative sponsorship deal made by any rugby league player worldwide.{{Citation needed|date=August 2008}} In March 2006, Sculthorpe signed a deal with [[Random House]] to publish his autobiography, ''Man of Steel'', which was published in August 2007.{{Citation needed|date=August 2008}} Sculthorpe captained St Helens as a {{rlp|sr}} in their [[2006 Challenge Cup]] Final victory against [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield]]. However his time as Great Britain captain was less successful, as he only ever captained the side for 18 minutes. This came in the one-off test game at [[Knowsley Road]] in 2006 between Great Britain and [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]], in which he was forced off after injuring his knee. Sculthorpe returned from the injury and was named man-of-the-match in St Helens' win over Brisbane Broncos in the [[2007 World Club Challenge]]. In September 2007 he signed a new 12-month contract with St Helens which saw him celebrate his testimonial season at the club. Sculthorpe's final game for St Helens was in the [[2008 Challenge Cup]] Final, where he injured his shoulder in the opening minutes. After being injury hit in 2008's [[Super League XIII]] with only a handful of games played, St. Helens announced they would release Sculthorpe at the end of the season. He was touted as a potential signing for [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]], or joining up with brother Danny at [[Wakefield Trinity]] in time for the 2009 Super League season.{{cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/st_helens/7559207.stm|title=St Helens to let Sculthorpe leave|publisher=BBC Sport|date=2008-08-13|access-date=13 August 2008}} However, Sculthorpe announced his retirement from the game on 19 September 2008.{{cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/st_helens/7626335.stm|title=Saints legend Sculthorpe retires|publisher=BBC Sport|date=2008-09-19|access-date=19 September 2008}} He was awarded the honour of [[Member of the Most Excellent Order of the British Empire|MBE]] in the 2013 New Year's Honours List, for "services to Rugby League and to charity". On 9 September 2024, Sculthorpe was inducted into the Rugby Football League Hall of Fame.{{cite web|url=https://www.sthelensstar.co.uk/sport/24571526.former-st-helens-captain-paul-sculthorpe-joins-hall-fame/|title=Former St Helens captain Paul Sculthorpe joins Hall of Fame|website=www.sthelensstar.co.uk|date=9 September 2024 }} ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *(archived by web.archive.org) [https://web.archive.org/web/20080107151035/http://www.saintsrlfc.com/player/13 Saints profile] *[http://www.saints.org.uk/saints/player.php?num=16305 Profile at saints.org.uk] *[http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/st_helens/7198849.stm Sculthorpe open to England return] *[http://www.engagepresenters.co.uk/paul_sculthorpe.html Paul Sculthorpe Official Website] *[http://www.myspace.com/paulsculthorpe Paul Sculthorpe Official Testimonial myspace] {{Navboxes |title= St Helens squads | bg=white | fg=red | bordercolor=red |list1= {{St Helens RLFC -
